samples.claim: The General Data Protection Regulation establishes this as a cornerstone of information privacy law.
The General Data Protection Regulation establishes this as a cornerstone of information privacy law.
The concept of personal data is broadly defined under the GDPR, encompassing any information relating to an identified or identifiable natural person.
**Penalty:** Non-compliance with Article 15 can result in fines up to €20 million or 4% of the undertaking's total annual worldwide turnover, whichever is higher, under Article 83(5).
**Legal context:** This right ensures data accuracy, a core principle of the GDPR under Article 5(1)(d) (the accuracy principle).
The Data Protection Directive (Directive 95/46/EC), which preceded the GDPR, also contained rectification rights under Article 12(b).

**Legal context:** The Right to be forgotten (RTBF) was established as a legal concept well before the GDPR, notably through the landmark case Google Spain v AEPD and Mario Costeja González (2014), wh

**Legal context:** The Right to be forgotten (RTBF) was established as a legal concept well before the GDPR, notably through the landmark case Google Spain v AEPD and Mario Costeja González (2014), wh
**Legal context:** Data portability is a relatively novel right introduced by the GDPR.
The ePrivacy Directive (Directive 2002/58/EC) complements the GDPR by providing specific rules on direct marketing communications and cookies.
**Legal context:** Automated decision-making is increasingly prevalent in areas like credit scoring, hiring, insurance, and targeted advertising.
The Artificial Intelligence Act (EU 2024) builds on these protections for high-risk AI systems.
The GDPR establishes a two-tier fine system under Article 83:
The Privacy law framework across the EU has been significantly strengthened by the GDPR, with Data Protection Authorities (DPAs) empowered to impose substantial fines.
The Schrems II decision (Case C-311/18) invalidated the Privacy Shield framework and imposed stricter requirements on transfers to third countries.
The Schrems II decision (Case C-311/18) invalidated the Privacy Shield framework and imposed stricter requirements on transfers to third countries.
The EU Data Protection Directive (Directive 95/46/EC) was the predecessor to the GDPR and established the initial framework for data subject rights in Europe.
The landmark Google Spain case established the right to be forgotten against search engines.
The Data portability article on Wikipedia provides additional context on implementation challenges.
The Information privacy law framework recognizes that data protection rights must be balanced against other fundamental rights and public interests.

tier_b_items.reason: Report merely lists rights without identifying patterns or trends.
No causal explanations; only describes what each right does.
No cross-referencing between different source types shown.
Only catalogs known rights; no synthesis producing novel insight.
No limitations, caveats, or counterarguments acknowledged.
No actionable recommendations; ends abruptly with catalog table.

verdicts: Does the catalog have >= 5 columns (name / demand / legal-ref / response-time / penalty)?
Does each row cite the GDPR article number (e.g. Art. 17 RTBF)?
Does each row cite >= 1 wiki URL?
Does each row cite >= 1 reddit URL with practitioner experience?
Does each row cite >= 1 shopping URL (book / handbook reference)?
Are exactly 3 sample request letter templates included (erasure / access / portability)?
Does each template cite a wiki article for legal basis?
Are >= 10 shopping product pages enumerated with price + rating?
Are >= 30 reddit threads from at least 4 EU/privacy sub-forums cited?
Are all 10 mandatory wiki articles (GDPR, RTBF, Data portability, DSAR, Personal data, Info privacy, Schrems II, Privacy Shield, EU DP Directive, ePrivacy) cited?
Does the report cite penalty examples (e.g. 4% global revenue or €20M, whichever higher)?
Are at least 3 real-world GDPR enforcement cases cited (e.g. Google, Amazon)?
Does the report distinguish controllers from processors?
Are all cited URLs markdown-linked and sandbox-resolvable?
Is per-domain minimum met: >= 10 shopping, >= 30 reddit, >= 25 wiki?
Does the report avoid producing a TOP-10 list?
Does the report explicitly cover non-EU residents' rights when their data is processed by EU companies?
